Proposed Claim: A large share of everyday behavior is repeated in the same context with little fresh decision-making.
Reader-Friendly Wording: Studies that follow people through the day suggest that much of what we do is repeated in familiar settings, often without a fresh decision each time.
Source Title: Habits in everyday life: Thought, emotion, and action
Authors: Wendy Wood; Jeffrey M. Quinn; Deborah A. Kashy
Publication: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology
Exact Supporting Passage: Diary studies: behaviors performed almost daily and in the same location were experienced as habitual (about 43% of behaviors in the samples).
Evidence Limitation: Diary self-report; community/student samples; correlational (describes patterns, not causes).

Proposed Claim: Habits are triggered automatically by context cues, rather than by a fresh conscious choice each time.
Reader-Friendly Wording: Research suggests many everyday actions are set off automatically by the situations and cues around us.
Authors: David T. Neal; Wendy Wood; Jeffrey M. Quinn
Exact Supporting Passage: “Habits are response dispositions that are activated automatically by the context cues that co-occurred with responses during past performance.”
Evidence Limitation: Review of prior work; describes mechanisms; not specific to spending.

Proposed Claim: Monitoring progress toward a goal is associated with a greater chance of reaching it.
Reader-Friendly Wording: A review of many experiments found that simply keeping track of your progress toward a goal is linked with a better chance of reaching it.
Source Title: Does monitoring goal progress promote goal attainment? A meta-analysis of the experimental evidence
Authors: Benjamin Harkin; Thomas L. Webb; Betty P. I. Chang; Andrew Prestwich; Mark Conner; Ian Kellar; Yael Benn; Paschal Sheeran
Exact Supporting Passage: Meta-analysis: prompting people to monitor goal progress had a significant effect on the rate of goal attainment.
Evidence Limitation: Effects vary; most studies were health/eating goals, so applying to spending is by analogy.

Proposed Claim: Specific if-then (“when-then”) plans help translate an intention into action better than a general intention.
Reader-Friendly Wording: Research suggests that deciding in advance what you will do in a specific situation — a “when-then” plan — is linked with a better chance of following through.
Source Title: Implementation Intentions and Goal Achievement: A Meta-analysis of Effects and Processes
Exact Supporting Passage: Meta-analysis across 94 studies: implementation intentions had a positive effect of medium-to-large size on goal attainment.
Evidence Limitation: Effect sizes vary by domain; averages across many goal types.

Proposed Claim: The default option (what happens unless you act) can strongly influence what people end up doing.
Reader-Friendly Wording: Research shows that the default — the choice that happens automatically unless you act — can strongly shape what people end up doing.
Authors: Eric J. Johnson; Daniel G. Goldstein
Exact Supporting Passage: “Default options can lead to striking differences in preferences, with significant economic impact.”
Evidence Limitation: Studied in organ-donation policy, not spending; illustrates the power of defaults generally.

Proposed Claim: Situational factors such as time to browse and in-the-moment feelings come before unplanned purchases.
Reader-Friendly Wording: One study found that things like having time to browse and how you feel in the moment tend to come before unplanned purchases.
Authors: Sharon E. Beatty; M. Elizabeth Ferrell
Exact Supporting Passage: Model of impulse buying: time available, in-store browsing, and positive feelings were precursors of impulse buying.
Evidence Limitation: Single study; mall shoppers in the 1990s; correlational model.

Proposed Claim: The design and feel of an online store can relate to unplanned online buying.
Reader-Friendly Wording: Research on online shopping suggests the design and feel of a store's website can relate to unplanned buying.
Source Title: The influence of online store beliefs on consumer online impulse buying: A model and empirical application
Exact Supporting Passage: Online store beliefs (e.g., enjoyment, ease of use) were related to consumers' online impulse buying in a structural model.
Evidence Limitation: Single study; one online retailer; correlational (SEM), not causal.

Proposed Claim: The discomfort people feel when spending, and how strongly they feel it, is linked to how much they spend; people differ widely.
Reader-Friendly Wording: Research describes how the discomfort some people feel when spending is linked to how much they spend — and people differ a lot in this.
Authors: Scott I. Rick; Cynthia E. Cryder; George Loewenstein
Exact Supporting Passage: “An anticipatory pain of paying drives ‘tightwads’ to spend less than they would ideally like to spend”; “spendthrifts” feel too little.
Evidence Limitation: Describes a measured trait; correlational; US samples.

Proposed Claim: Worrying about money can occupy mental attention, which may make other things harder to focus on.
Reader-Friendly Wording: Some research suggests that worrying about money can take up mental attention, which may make it harder to notice or focus on other things.
Source Title: Some Consequences of Having Too Little
Authors: Anuj K. Shah; Sendhil Mullainathan; Eldar Shafir
Exact Supporting Passage: “Scarcity” of resources can change how people attend to and engage with problems (companion work: Mani et al., 2013).
Evidence Limitation: Studied in scarcity / low-income contexts; a re-analysis (Wicherts & Zand Scholten, 2013) questioned parts of Mani et al.; do not assume any reader is poor.

Proposed Claim: Responding to a slip with self-kindness rather than harsh criticism is associated with more motivation to improve.
Reader-Friendly Wording: Research suggests that responding to a slip with self-kindness, rather than harsh self-criticism, is linked with more motivation to try again.
Exact Supporting Passage: Across four experiments, a self-compassion condition increased self-improvement motivation after a mistake or weakness.
Evidence Limitation: Lab experiments; mostly student samples; not about money specifically.

Proposed Claim: Self-compassion is associated with lower stress when people fall short of their intentions.
Reader-Friendly Wording: Research on self-compassion suggests it is associated with lower stress when we fall short of what we intended.
Source Title: Procrastination and Stress: Exploring the Role of Self-compassion
Exact Supporting Passage: Self-compassion mediated the link between procrastination and stress: higher self-compassion was associated with lower stress.

Proposed Claim: Forming a new habit takes time and varies widely; missing a single day does not prevent it.
Reader-Friendly Wording: In one real-world study, a new habit took about two months on average to feel automatic — with a wide range — and missing a single day did not stop it from forming.
Source Title: How are habits formed: Modelling habit formation in the real world
Authors: Phillippa Lally; Cornelia H. M. van Jaarsveld; Henry W. W. Potts; Jane Wardle
Exact Supporting Passage: Median time to reach the automaticity plateau was 66 days (range 18--254); “missing one opportunity to perform the behaviour did not materially affect the habit formation process.”
Evidence Limitation: Small sample (82 analyzed); self-report habit index; simple eating/drinking/activity behaviors.

Proposed Claim: People may keep paying for subscriptions they under-use, sometimes paying more than a pay-per-use option.
Reader-Friendly Wording: One study of gym memberships found many people chose monthly plans but visited so rarely they paid much more than paying per visit — and some kept paying after they stopped going.
Source Title: Paying Not to Go to the Gym
Exact Supporting Passage: Members who chose a monthly contract paid on average over $17 per visit when a per-visit option existed; many delayed cancellation after stopping attendance.
Evidence Limitation: Gym-membership context; early-2000s US data; specific market.

Proposed Claim: Subscriptions often renew automatically unless canceled; knowing the terms and how to cancel helps.
Reader-Friendly Wording: A consumer guide from the Federal Trade Commission explains that subscriptions often renew automatically unless you cancel, and suggests knowing the terms and how to cancel before signing up.
Source Title: Getting In and Out of Free Trials, Auto-Renewals, and Negative Option Subscriptions
Exact Supporting Passage: “Some will auto-renew if you don't cancel first”; advises knowing the terms, watching for pre-checked boxes, and noting how to cancel.
Evidence Limitation: Consumer-education guidance, not a research study.

Proposed Claim: Financial well-being is a personal state that includes security and freedom of choice, and it looks different for each person.
Reader-Friendly Wording: The Consumer Financial Protection Bureau describes financial well-being as being able to meet your obligations, feel secure, and have the freedom to make choices — and notes it looks different for each person.
Source Title: Financial well-being: The goal of financial education
Exact Supporting Passage: Report proposes a definition of financial well-being “grounded in the existing literature, expert opinion, and the … voice of the consumer.”
Evidence Limitation: A framework/definition, not evidence that any exercise raises well-being.

Proposed Claim: Confidence in one's ability to do something (self-efficacy) influences how much effort people put in and how long they persist.
Reader-Friendly Wording: A foundational psychology theory proposes that our confidence in being able to do something affects how much effort we put in and how long we keep going.
Source Title: Self-efficacy: Toward a unifying theory of behavioral change
Exact Supporting Passage: Self-efficacy theory: perceived self-efficacy affects initiation of behavior, effort expended, and persistence in the face of obstacles.
